tools.name: Claude Code
tools.tagline: Anthropic's agentic coding tool that writes and ships real software from your terminal.
ai4ntp_take: Claude Code is the workhorse behind our builds, and all four operators use it daily. In Episode 002, Alec Saluga used it to build GotoBuild's working AI workout-plan generator live, and BrandSauce.io's entire backend runs on it. Justin Novak uses it as the contrast case against an always-on OpenClaw agent in Episode 003, as his daily driver for hands-on building inside VS Code in Episode 004, and again in Episode 005 to build the Milk and Cookies marketplace flow from a spoken, plain-English prompt.

We keep it in the stack because it closes the gap between an idea and a working build. It doesn't just write code, it runs commands and ships changes, so a founder who can describe a feature in plain English can watch it get built in real time. You don't need to write code yourself to benefit from Claude Code. You need someone, or some AI, driving it who can describe what you want clearly.

episodes.used_for: Custom development and backend work
Building the working workout-plan generator live
Serving as his daily coding driver, contrasted against an always-on OpenClaw agent
Serving as his daily driver for hands-on building inside VS Code
Building the Milk and Cookies marketplace flow inside VS Code from a spoken, plain-English prompt
Building the first iteration of the Saints Classical LMS

pricing.summary: Paid; included with Claude paid plans / usage-based.

what_is: Claude Code is Anthropic's agentic coding tool that runs from your terminal. Instead of just suggesting snippets in a chat window, it reads your actual codebase, edits files, runs commands, and carries out multi-step engineering work on its own. You describe what you want built or fixed, and it works through the project directly, the way a developer would.

It's a paid tool, either bundled into a Claude subscription or billed by usage, and it's built for people who already write code. You don't need to write code yourself to benefit from what it builds, but running it directly assumes you're comfortable in a terminal or code editor.
tools.who_for: Claude Code lives in a terminal or a code editor, which makes it look like a developer tool, because it is one. But on the show, it's proven itself as something you can direct successfully even without writing code yourself: describe what you want in plain English, and it handles writing, running, and fixing the code. You still need someone comfortable opening a terminal or VS Code and giving clear instructions, whether that's you, an operator on your team, or the AI running it for you.

tools.name: Codex
tools.tagline: OpenAI's coding agent for building and shipping apps.
ai4ntp_take: Codex is Justin's pick. On the Episode 001 live he said the entire AI4NTP site, the ads, and the interactive widget were built with Codex. Alec has used it too: in Episode 004 he had Codex pull from the SpyFu API to build a prospect list in about 10 minutes, and in Episode 005 he tested it as one coding-agent path before choosing Lovable for that build.

We keep Codex in the stack because it handles real, multi-file projects inside a tool most operators already have open (ChatGPT), not some separate technical setup. You do not need to know how to code to direct it: you describe the outcome, Codex writes and runs the code, and you review what comes back. Weigh it against Claude Code if you want a second opinion, but for about $20 a month, a tool that shipped an entire live site has earned its spot here.

episodes.used_for: Building the AI4NTP site, ads, and live widget
Building a prospect list via the SpyFu API in about 10 minutes
Comparing coding-agent options before choosing Lovable
Driving an Apify scraper and enriching prospect data for the cold-email agent

pricing.summary: Paid; ~$20/mo (stated on the live).

what_is: Codex is OpenAI's coding agent, available through ChatGPT, that writes, edits, and ships code from plain-language instructions. You describe what you want built or fixed, and Codex works in a sandboxed environment to generate the code, run it, and report back.
It handles full projects, not just snippets, so it can scaffold a website, wire up a form, or fix a bug across multiple files. Pricing runs roughly $20 a month, with no free tier.
tools.who_for: Codex is built for writing and shipping code, which means what it produces under the hood is technical, and getting a project fully live still involves things like hosting and deployment. You do not have to write that code yourself, though. You describe what you want in plain language, and Codex handles the execution. If you are comfortable directing an assistant and reviewing what it builds, Codex is workable without a programming background. That is how Justin used it to build the AI4NTP site, ads, and widget.

tools.name: Lovable
tools.tagline: Vibe-coding website builder that turns prompts into live sites.
ai4ntp_take: Alec Saluga treats Lovable as his default builder whenever a project needs a live site fast. In Episode 001, he used it for landing pages and prototypes while exploring how marketers actually use AI to grow. In Episode 002, building the AI workout planner GotoBuild in under an hour, he used it to build and compare the GotoBuild marketing site. In Episode 005, building three real companies in one hour, including the bakery Milk and Cookies, he used it for the Crumb B2B landing page, the cookie-drop order flow, and a publishable app prototype.

We keep Lovable in the stack because it's the lowest-friction place to start if you've never built anything before, and it still turns out a clean site or landing page fast. The newer Lovable Cloud layer adds Supabase-backed storage and login under the hood, which matters once a prototype needs real users or real data. The takeaway: if you have an idea and a deadline, describe it to Lovable before you go looking for a developer.

episodes.used_for: Landing pages and prototypes
Building and comparing the GotoBuild marketing site
Building the Crumb B2B landing page, cookie-drop order flow, and publishable app prototype

pricing.summary: Free tier available; paid plans for more usage.

what_is: Lovable is a vibe-coding platform: you describe what you want in plain English, and it generates a working website, app, or product from that prompt. Instead of writing code, you describe layouts, features, and copy in a chat interface and watch the site update live.

It handles both the front end (the pages people see) and, through its Lovable Cloud feature, backend basics like storage and login, powered by Supabase under the hood. That means you can go from an idea to a live, working site without hiring a developer or touching a code editor.
tools.who_for: Lovable is built for people who have never written code and don't plan to start, more so than most tools in this directory. You describe what you want in everyday language, and it builds and hosts the working site for you, so the main skill you need is explaining clearly what you want. If you're choosing your first AI tool to build with, this is one of the easiest on-ramps in the directory.

tools.name: Manus
tools.tagline: No-code AI agent platform for building real, functional apps.
ai4ntp_take: Ian Kilpatrick uses Manus as his go-to when a project needs to actually function, not just look finished. In Episode 001, on how marketers are actually using AI to grow, he recommends it specifically for building functional apps without writing code, the moments when a static page or a chatbot reply isn't enough and you need something that runs.

We keep it in the stack because most operators hit that exact wall: "make me a page" turns into "make me a tool that does something." Manus is built for that shift. It won't replace a website builder for polishing a landing page, that's a job for something like Lovable, but when you need a working app, an automated workflow, or a small piece of software and you don't have a developer on call, it gets you there without touching code. The takeaway: if your idea only works as something live and functioning, not a static screen, this is the tool to reach for first.

episodes.used_for: Building functional apps no-code

pricing.summary: Credit-based plans; see site.

what_is: Manus is a no-code AI agent platform built to execute tasks, not just answer questions. Instead of returning text, it acts: writing code, building functional apps, automating multi-step workflows, and completing jobs end to end so you don't have to do them by hand.

You describe what you want in plain language, and Manus works through the steps to build it. It runs on credit-based plans with a free tier to start, so you can test what it can do before committing to a paid plan.
tools.who_for: Manus is more approachable than a raw coding tool since you describe what you want in plain language, but it's still an agent that writes and runs real code behind the scenes, so expect a learning curve beyond basic chat tools. It suits operators comfortable experimenting, testing, and iterating on prompts rather than expecting instant, polished results the first time. If you want function over a pretty page and don't want to hire a developer, this is where you start.

tools.name: Supabase
tools.tagline: Open-source backend-as-a-service: database, auth, and APIs.
ai4ntp_take: Ian Kilpatrick used Supabase as the backend behind a live app on Episode 001, and it now also runs quietly behind the scenes on AI4NTP's own session pages, powering the live audience pulse feature. Ian has said flatly that it's a tool he underestimated at first and now uses religiously.

That's the practical case for keeping Supabase in the stack: once a project needs real user accounts, saved data, or live updates instead of static pages, it needs a backend, and Supabase gets you one without months of setup. You likely won't log into it yourself, but the AI tools and developers you work with will. Knowing it exists helps you ask for the right thing: asking someone to wire a project to a database is now a reasonable request, not a moonshot.

episodes.used_for: Backend for live apps, powering AI4NTP's audience pulse feature

pricing.summary: Generous free tier; usage-based paid plans.

what_is: Supabase is an open-source backend-as-a-service built on top of Postgres, one of the most widely used databases in the world. Instead of hiring engineers to build a database, login system, and APIs from scratch, developers get all of it out of the box: a full Postgres database, user authentication, auto-generated APIs, real-time data sync, serverless functions, file storage, and support for AI vector embeddings.

It's commonly described as an open-source alternative to Firebase, aimed at teams who want more control over their data while still moving fast. Supabase offers a free tier to start, with paid plans that scale based on usage.
tools.who_for: Supabase is a developer tool. Setting it up means writing code, designing database tables, and configuring authentication rules, so if you're not building software yourself, you won't be the one logging into it. It's still worth knowing what it does: when an AI tool or a developer you hire says a project needs "a backend" or "a database," Supabase is very often what they mean, and it's the difference between an app that scales and one that breaks under real use.

tools.name: OpenClaw
tools.tagline: Self-hosted AI agent you run like a digital employee.
ai4ntp_take: Ian runs roughly a dozen OpenClaw agents from Telegram. In Episode 003, he set one up live on camera, starting from a blank server: hardened it, installed a model, and connected it to Telegram in under an hour. Pointed at the Episode 002 brief, an OpenClaw agent had already produced GotoBuild's full hosted brand kit. In Episode 005, he ran multiple OpenClaw agents in parallel to build Sunday Crumb, handling the branding, site copy, order flow, and admin view all at once.

We keep OpenClaw in the stack because it proves an agent can act like staff, not just answer a question once and stop working. The practical takeaway here isn't to go harden a server yourself. It's that a working AI employee is one blank server and about an hour away, and once it's running, you manage it the same way you'd message anyone else on your team.

episodes.used_for: Producing GotoBuild's full hosted brand kit
Setting one up live, from a blank server to a working Telegram bot
Running multiple parallel agents for Sunday Crumb's branding, copy, orders, and admin view
Named agents (Atlas, Saint, Saucy) that built Echo Check, run the Saints Classical LMS, and give every BrandSauce employee their own agent

pricing.summary: Free and open source (MIT license). You pay only for your server and your model.

what_is: OpenClaw is a self-hosted AI agent you run on your own server rather than a hosted app you log into. It is free and open source under the MIT license, so the only real costs are the server it runs on and whichever AI model you connect to it.
Once it is set up, you can wire it into a channel like Telegram, so you message it directly and it works through tasks on its own, more like an employee than a piece of software you open.
tools.who_for: OpenClaw itself is a technical tool: setting it up means provisioning a server, hardening it, and connecting a model and a channel like Telegram. That's real infrastructure work, not a beginner-friendly signup flow. It's worth knowing about anyway, because it's what proves an AI agent can run like an actual employee instead of a chat window. Most operators will have someone else (or, increasingly, another AI) do the setup, then talk to the finished agent like a coworker.

tools.name: Zapier
tools.tagline: Automation glue that connects your apps and AI into workflows.
ai4ntp_take: Alec Saluga runs this quietly in the background. In Episode 004, he set up Zapier to push his PLAUD Note recordings into Google Drive automatically. From there, in Episode 001, those transcripts flow into Google Docs and then into Claude, which turns them into proposals and follow-ups. Zapier is the connective tissue in that chain. It does not write the copy or record the meeting, it just makes sure nothing has to be moved by hand between the tools that do.

That is exactly why it stays in the stack. Zapier is boring by design, and that is the point: it is the layer that keeps a workflow from breaking when one tool hands off to the next. The takeaway is not to master every Zapier feature. Pick the one repetitive handoff you do most, exporting a file, forwarding a transcript, logging a lead, and let a single Zap do it automatically. One reliable connection beats a dozen manual steps.

episodes.used_for: Automating transcript-to-AI workflows
Pushing PLAUD recordings into Google Drive automatically

pricing.summary: Free tier available; paid plans by task volume.

what_is: Zapier is an automation platform that connects the apps you already use, from Google Docs to Slack to Claude, so tasks move between them automatically instead of you copying and pasting by hand. You set up what Zapier calls a "Zap": a trigger in one app, like a new file landing in a folder, that fires an action in another, like sending that file to an AI tool.

Zapier connects more than 9,000 apps and is used by millions of businesses to build these workflows without writing code. Increasingly, it is also used to string AI models directly into the automation, so a workflow can pull in a large language model as one more step in the chain.
tools.who_for: Zapier is built for people who do not write code, and that is the honest pitch: you connect apps through dropdown menus and simple if-this-then-that logic, not scripts. The learning curve is real but small. Your first automation might take an afternoon of trial and error to get the trigger and action talking to each other correctly. Once one Zap works, though, copying the pattern to your next workflow is fast. If you can follow a recipe, you can build a Zap.

tools.name: Pulsar
tools.tagline: Multi-tenant content engine that turns a brand kit into a marketing motion.
ai4ntp_take: Justin built Pulsar himself using Claude Code, and it's the engine behind AI4NTP's own go-to-market work. In Episode 002, the crew built a company with AI in under an hour (the voted brief was GotoBuild, an AI workout planner), and Justin ran GotoBuild's entire go-to-market through Pulsar. The newsletter, the blog post, and the social content all came out of the same brand kit and voice setup, without rewriting the message channel by channel.

We keep Pulsar in the stack because it proves a point we make constantly: one clear brand kit, fed into the right system, can drive a newsletter, a blog, and social copy without starting from scratch on each one. You don't need Pulsar itself to get that benefit. The real takeaway is the workflow behind it: define your voice once, then let one system (or one disciplined process) push it out everywhere, instead of rewriting your message for every channel you touch.

episodes.used_for: Running GotoBuild's go-to-market across newsletter, blog, and social

pricing.summary: Private; not publicly available.

what_is: Pulsar is a multi-tenant content engine that turns a brand kit into a marketing motion. You give it a brand's voice, tone, and key messaging, and it drafts a newsletter, then waterfalls that same content into blog posts and social copy, all matched to the brand's voice.

Pulsar was built to run more than one brand at a time, so a single system can manage content pipelines for several accounts instead of just one. It's a private, custom-built tool, not a public product with its own signup or pricing page.
tools.who_for: Pulsar isn't something you can sign up for. It's Justin's own internal build, not a public product, so there's no plan to buy or account to create; if you want access, or want something similar built for your brand, email Justin directly. The workflow it automates, turning one brand kit into a newsletter, blog, and social content, is worth understanding even if you never touch Pulsar itself, since it's a pattern you can apply with off-the-shelf tools or a comparable custom build.

tools.name: ChatGPT
tools.tagline: OpenAI's general-purpose AI assistant for writing, images, and code.
ai4ntp_take: We keep reaching for ChatGPT because it covers a lot of ground. In Episode 002, Ian Kilpatrick generated the first logo for GotoBuild, the AI workout planner, from a Midjourney reference image. Justin Novak flipped that workflow in Episode 004, having ChatGPT write a Midjourney prompt from an uploaded photo. In Episode 003, ChatGPT 5.5 (GPT-5) was the model brain running Ian's live OpenClaw agent, included with his subscription. And in Episode 005, Ian and Alec used it to ideate the bakery concepts Sunday Crumb and Crumb B2B, and to compare image outputs against Higgsfield.

We keep it in the stack because it shows up in almost every build, whether that's a quick image reference, an agent's model brain, or a thinking partner for naming a new company. If you're new to AI, ChatGPT is the reasonable default: general enough for writing, images, or code, and the fastest way to see what a model can do for your work.

episodes.used_for: Generating the first GotoBuild logo from a Midjourney reference
Serving as the model brain behind the live OpenClaw agent
Generating a Midjourney prompt from an uploaded image
Ideating Sunday Crumb and Crumb B2B, and comparing image outputs in Higgsfield
Referenced as the answer engine now recommending and sending clients to BrandSauce

pricing.summary: Free tier; Plus ~$20/mo (stated on the live).

what_is: ChatGPT is OpenAI's general-purpose AI assistant for writing, research, images, and code. You describe what you need in plain language, in a back-and-forth chat, and it responds with drafts, answers, generated images, or working code. It runs in a browser, desktop app, or mobile app, with no setup beyond creating an account.

There's a free tier, plus a paid Plus plan for higher usage limits and access to newer models. Under the hood it's powered by OpenAI's GPT models, the same family of AI that underlies many other products.
tools.who_for: ChatGPT is one of the more approachable AI tools out there. You don't install anything or write code: you sign up, open a chat window, and type what you want in plain English, the same way you'd text a very capable assistant. If you've never used AI before, this is a reasonable place to start, since it can draft writing, answer questions, generate images, and even explain code back to you in plain language, all from one app.

tools.name: Perplexity
tools.tagline: AI answer engine for research and step-by-step walkthroughs.
ai4ntp_take: In Episode 002, where Ian Kilpatrick built GotoBuild, an AI workout planner, in under an hour, Perplexity was the tool he named at the close as his go-to for research and for getting clear, step-by-step instructions. When you're moving that fast on a build, you don't have time to sift through ten search results and piece together your own answer; you need one clean explanation with the reasoning already worked out.

That's why it stays in the stack: when a build hits a decision point, like which approach to take or how something actually works, Perplexity gives you one direct, sourced answer instead of a dozen browser tabs to reconcile. The practical takeaway is to make it your first stop any time you need to research something quickly or want a process explained in plain, ordered steps, before you go digging anywhere else.

episodes.used_for: Research and step-by-step walkthroughs
Referenced as an AI answer engine to optimize visibility for

pricing.summary: Free tier; Pro plan for advanced models.

what_is: Perplexity is an AI answer engine built for research. Instead of handing you a list of links like a traditional search engine, it searches the web in real time and writes a direct, cited answer you can check against the sources. You can keep asking follow-up questions in the same thread, and it pulls fresh information each time rather than relying only on what it already knows.
It's also strong at step-by-step instructions, laying out a process in order instead of burying it in a paragraph. Perplexity has a free tier, plus a paid Pro plan for more advanced models.
tools.who_for: Perplexity is one of the easier AI tools to pick up if you've ever used a search engine. You type a question in plain English, get a direct answer with sources listed below it, and ask follow-ups the same way you would in a chat. There's no setup and no prompt-writing skill required, and the free tier covers most everyday research. If you already Google things, you already know how to use this.

tools.name: Wispr Flow
tools.tagline: AI voice dictation that lets you prompt by talking instead of typing.
ai4ntp_take: Alec uses Wispr Flow daily to prompt his AI tools by voice instead of typing. In Episode 002, he used it to voice-prompt the tools that built GotoBuild, an AI workout planner, in under an hour. In Episode 004, Justin ran voice dictation with live auto-correct throughout a session testing seven lesser-known AI tools. In Episode 005, Justin and Alec both dictated the long prompts covering brand, product, and Lovable build context for three companies built live in one hour, including the bakery brand Milk and Cookies.

We keep it in the stack because prompting AI well takes long, specific instructions, and typing them out tempts you to shortcut the detail. Talking doesn't. Flow lets you think out loud while it cleans up your stumbles into a finished paragraph. The takeaway for any operator: if your prompts are short because typing them is tedious, voice removes that excuse, and it works with any AI tool, not just one.

episodes.used_for: Voice-prompting AI tools to build an app in under an hour
Voice dictation with live auto-correct throughout the session
Dictating long prompts for brand, product, and Lovable build context

pricing.summary: Free tier available; paid plan for more usage.

what_is: Wispr Flow is a voice dictation app that turns speech into clean, formatted text in real time. Instead of typing, you talk, and Flow transcribes what you say while auto-correcting mistakes and adjusting punctuation as you go.

It works inside the apps you already use, including email, documents, chat, and the prompt boxes of AI tools, so anywhere you would normally type, you can speak instead. Wispr Flow offers a free tier, with a paid plan for heavier use.
tools.who_for: Wispr Flow is built for anyone who writes on a computer, not just developers or power users. If you can talk, you can use it: install it, hit a shortcut, and speak instead of type. It is especially useful if you use AI tools regularly, since writing good prompts means writing a lot, and dictating is faster and more natural than typing paragraphs by hand. No coding or technical setup is required beyond installing the app.

tools.name: Midjourney
tools.tagline: Text-to-image model for logos, moodboards, and visual direction.
ai4ntp_take: Ian used Midjourney to generate the logo and visual moodboard for GotoBuild, the AI workout planner built live in Episode 002. Justin has used it too. In Episode 004, on 7 AI tools you have never heard of, he used it for creative ideation and prompt-to-image work on merch and site mockups. In Episode 005, building three real companies in one hour including the bakery Milk and Cookies, he used it to generate the early logo and brand directions for that brand.

We keep Midjourney in the stack because it's still the fastest way to go from a one-line idea to a wall of visual directions you can react to. The practical takeaway if you are not a designer: the value isn't in learning prompt syntax, it's in speed. You can react to a handful of logo or brand directions in the time it would take to brief a human designer, then hand the one you like off to someone to finish properly.

episodes.used_for: Logo and visual moodboarding for the GotoBuild brand build
Creative ideation and prompt-to-image work for merch and site mockups
Generating early logo and brand directions for Milk and Cookies

pricing.summary: Paid; ~$29/mo on the tier Ian used (limited free trial).

what_is: Midjourney is a text-to-image AI model. You type a written description and it generates a set of image options built from it, usually through Discord or Midjourney's own web app.

It's one of the most widely used tools for concept art, logo ideas, and mood boards, giving you a fast way to explore what a brand or product could look like before a designer touches it. Midjourney has historically leaned toward stylized, painterly output over strict photorealism, which makes it especially good for direction-setting work: logos, moodboards, and visual identity, rather than finished production assets.
tools.who_for: Midjourney is a genuinely approachable tool. There's no coding involved: you type what you want to see in plain English and get images back. The one real hurdle is the interface: Midjourney runs through Discord (or its own web app now), which can feel unfamiliar the first time you open it. Once you're past that, prompting is closer to describing a vision to a designer than programming a computer, so it's a strong fit if you want fast visual direction without hiring one.

tools.name: Behance
tools.tagline: Adobe's portfolio network for design inspiration and trend-reading.
ai4ntp_take: Ian opens Behance first when a brand needs direction. In Episode 002, building GotoBuild (an AI workout planner) from a live audience brief in under an hour, he used it for design inspiration and trend-reading before any assets got made. Justin does the same on the content side: in Episode 004 he named it alongside Midjourney as a creative-inspiration gallery, and in Episode 005, shaping the brand direction for the bakery Milk and Cookies, he browsed it for visual inspiration before locking in a look.

Behance stays in the stack because it's a reality check on taste. AI tools can generate dozens of brand options, but they don't know what's overused or what a given industry's design language looks like right now. Behance does. The practical takeaway: before you prompt an AI tool for a logo, palette, or brand look, spend ten minutes browsing Behance in your category first. You'll walk in with a clearer sense of what good looks like and get sharper output because you can describe it.

episodes.used_for: Design inspiration and trend-reading
A creative-inspiration gallery referenced alongside Midjourney
Finding visual inspiration while exploring Milk and Cookies' brand direction

pricing.summary: Free.

what_is: Behance is Adobe's online portfolio network, a place where designers, illustrators, photographers, and other creative professionals publish finished projects for anyone to browse. Instead of stock photos or templates, you're looking at real work: full brand identity systems, packaging concepts, app interfaces, illustration series, organized by category, industry, and style.

It's free to browse, and free to create an account if you want to save projects or follow specific creators. Most people use it the way you'd flip through a design magazine: not to build anything inside it, but to see what good work in a given space actually looks like.
tools.who_for: Behance is about as simple as tools get: there's nothing to configure and no workflow to learn. You search or browse by category, and you look at what comes up. You don't need a design background to use it, only an eye for what you're drawn to. It's most useful in the ten minutes before you start a brand or design project, as a way to gather reference and figure out what "good" looks like in your space before you touch an AI tool.

tools.name: Pexels
tools.tagline: Free, rights-clear stock photography.
what_is: Pexels is a free stock photo and video library. Every asset is published under the Pexels License, which means you can download it, use it commercially, and edit it without paying a fee or crediting the photographer. There is no watermark, and you do not need an account to download.

The library is curated rather than endless, so the average quality is high and the results skew modern and editorial. Pexels also offers a free API, and it has been part of Canva since 2019, which is why many of the same photos show up inside Canva's editor.
ai4ntp_take: Ian Kilpatrick reached for Pexels during the Episode 002 build, when the room voted to create GotoBuild, an AI workout planner, in under an hour. Before touching layout, he used Pexels to set photo direction: pulling real, rights-clear imagery that showed how the brand should feel, so the visual direction was grounded in actual photography instead of guesswork.

We keep Pexels in the stack because it removes the two things that slow a fast brand build down: cost and licensing risk. When you are shipping a real company the same day you name it, you cannot stop to clear rights on every image. Pexels is free and commercial-safe by default, so the photo you drop into a hero section at minute forty is a photo you can legally keep.
tools.who_for: Yes. Pexels is about as non-technical as a tool gets. You type what you want, download the result, and use it, with no license to decode, no account to create, and nothing to install. If you have ever hesitated to use an image because you were not sure you were allowed to, Pexels removes that question entirely.

episodes.used_for: Photo direction on the GotoBuild brand

pricing.summary: Free. Every photo and video is covered by the Pexels License, including for commercial use.

tools.name: Google Fonts
tools.tagline: Free, open-source web type library.
ai4ntp_take: In Episode 002, where the team built a company with AI in under an hour (the voted brief was GotoBuild, an AI workout planner), Ian Kilpatrick used Google Fonts as the brand's type system. He reached for Archivo, a clean geometric sans, to give GotoBuild a polished look fast, without commissioning custom type or paying for a licensed font.

We keep Google Fonts in the stack because it removes an entire category of friction: no licensing questions, no invoices, no waiting on a designer to hand over font files. Every family works instantly in a browser and drops cleanly into a website's code or any design tool that accepts font files. The practical takeaway for an operator building a brand fast: typography is one of the quickest ways to make an AI-built brand look intentional, and Google Fonts lets you make that call in minutes instead of treating it as an afterthought.

episodes.used_for: Choosing Archivo as the type system for GotoBuild's brand

pricing.summary: Free.

what_is: Google Fonts is a free, open-source library of web typefaces hosted by Google. It holds a large, constantly growing collection of font families, from classic serifs to modern sans-serifs, and licenses every one of them for free commercial and personal use, with the stated goal of making the web more beautiful, fast, and open through good typography.

You browse the library by style or name, preview any font with your own text, and either link to it directly in a website's code or download the files to use elsewhere. It's a standard, no-cost way to give a brand a clean, professional look.
tools.who_for: Google Fonts is one of the easiest tools in this directory to use. You don't need to know any code to browse the library, preview a typeface with real text, and decide what fits a brand. Picking a font is entirely a visual, click-and-preview decision. The one technical step, actually wiring a font into a live website, is usually handled by your site builder, developer, or the AI doing the build, so you can focus on choosing the look and never touch the code yourself.

tools.name: Brand Sauce
tools.tagline: Custom company swag stores for $1 a month.
ai4ntp_take: Brand Sauce is Ian Kilpatrick's own company, and it's the live case study behind his programmatic SEO breakdown in Episode 001. Ian used Brand Sauce's actual site to walk through an 8,000-page programmatic SEO deploy that took the site from zero indexed pages to thousands, showing how the strategy performs on a real production business instead of a slide deck.

We keep Brand Sauce in the stack because it's proof, not theory: a real company running the same SEO tactics we teach, at production scale. For an operator, the lesson isn't to go build 8,000 pages overnight. It's that programmatic SEO is learnable and repeatable once you watch someone execute it on a live business, and Brand Sauce is the example we point to when we want to show what that looks like in practice.

episodes.used_for: Programmatic SEO case study
Showing the AEO/GEO results that took the site from about 26 indexed pages to 14,530, and the SaucyBot agent that gives every employee their own AI

pricing.summary: From $1/mo (stated on the live).

what_is: Brand Sauce is a platform for building custom, branded company swag stores. It gives a company access to more than a million premium products, on-demand fulfillment, and no order minimums, so a team can launch its own storefront without holding inventory or managing shipping.
Setup and support are handled end to end, from building the store to getting orders to each recipient's doorstep. Plans start at $1 a month.
tools.who_for: Brand Sauce isn't really an AI tool you sit down and use, it's a swag store platform, and the AI4NTP angle is watching how it was built and grown, specifically the programmatic SEO strategy behind it. If you run a company and want a branded swag store, Brand Sauce is straightforward: pick products, launch a store, done. If you're here to learn programmatic SEO, treat Brand Sauce as the case study to study, not a tool to sign up for.

tools.name: Beehiiv
tools.tagline: Newsletter platform where you own your audience.
ai4ntp_take: Beehiiv is where Justin ships AI4NTP's own newsletter, and it's wired directly into our signup pipeline, so someone who joins the list lands in a system built to send and grow from. It's also proven on air: in Episode 002, when AI4NTP built GotoBuild, an AI workout planner, from scratch in under an hour, Justin used Beehiiv to ship GotoBuild's first newsletter live in front of the audience.

We keep Beehiiv in the stack because it handles the unglamorous parts of running a newsletter, list management, sending, and basic growth tools, without requiring anyone to touch code or stitch together separate services. A newsletter is one of the fastest ways to own an audience instead of renting one from a platform, and Episode 002 proved Beehiiv can get a working list and site live fast, on camera, in under an hour.

episodes.used_for: Shipping the GotoBuild newsletter live on air

pricing.summary: Free tier available; paid plans for scale.

what_is: Beehiiv is a newsletter platform built for creating, growing, and monetizing an email newsletter from one dashboard. You write and send issues, manage your subscriber list, and track opens and clicks without touching any code. It also includes built-in growth tools, like referral programs and a network for cross-promoting with other newsletters, plus ways to monetize directly through paid subscriptions or ads.

Beehiiv can host a simple website for your newsletter too, so you don't need a separate site builder. The pitch is ownership: your list and your content live on a platform you control, not rented space inside someone else's algorithm.
tools.who_for: Beehiiv is built for people who write, not people who code. If you can write an email, you can run a newsletter on Beehiiv: the editor, subscriber list, and send tools are all point-and-click, and the free tier lets you start without committing to a paid plan. It's the right fit for solo creators, small businesses, and anyone who wants a direct line to their audience instead of depending on social platforms to show up in someone's feed.

tools.name: Echo Check
tools.tagline: LLM visibility scoring: see how you rank in ChatGPT, Perplexity, Claude, and Gemini.
ai4ntp_take: Ian Kilpatrick built Echo Check. In Episode 001, on how marketers are actually using AI to grow, he used it to measure his own LLM and answer-engine visibility. He pointed the tool at a site, let it scan and score how the brand showed up across ChatGPT, Perplexity, Claude, and Gemini, and used that score as a read on whether the AI actually knew the business and would recommend it.

We keep Echo Check in the stack because it was built for marketers, not engineers: no code, no setup, just a domain in and a score out. The free plan covers most use cases, so there's no reason not to check your own number before spending more time or budget chasing AI visibility. The practical takeaway: if you don't know your Echo Check score, you don't know how ChatGPT, Perplexity, Claude, and Gemini are already describing your business to the people asking them for a recommendation.

episodes.used_for: Measuring LLM / answer-engine visibility
Demoed live: scoring AI visibility and getting BrandSauce recommended by ChatGPT

pricing.summary: Free plan covers most use cases.

what_is: Echo Check scores how a brand shows up when people ask AI assistants for recommendations instead of searching Google. Point it at your website, and it checks how ChatGPT, Perplexity, Claude, and Gemini describe your business, whether they recommend you at all, and how you compare to competitors in the same space.

That produces a visibility score you can track over time, the same way you'd track search rankings, but for the AI answer engines more people are turning to instead of typing a query into a browser. Echo Check runs on a free plan that covers most use cases.
tools.who_for: Echo Check is built for marketers, not engineers. You give it a website, it scans and scores automatically, and you read the result the way you'd read any ranking report, no code and no technical setup required. If you already care about SEO or brand mentions, this is the same instinct applied to ChatGPT, Perplexity, Claude, and Gemini. The free plan covers most use cases, so it costs nothing to check your own score first.

tools.name: Apollo.io
tools.tagline: Lead and contact database for prospecting.
ai4ntp_take: When a cold-outreach question came up during the Episode 002 Q&A, Alec Saluga pointed to Apollo.io as the off-the-shelf answer rather than something to build from scratch. He uses it for prospect and contact data, pulling verified emails for the accounts he wants to reach, so outreach starts with a real contact instead of a guessed inbox.

We keep Apollo.io in the stack because it removes the one step nobody wants to build by hand: a clean, current database of contacts. That means no scraper and no developer. You search Apollo's filters, export the list, and load it straight into your outreach tool. Start on the free tier to see whether the data actually moves a campaign before paying for more credits.

episodes.used_for: Sourcing prospect and contact data for cold outreach

pricing.summary: Free tier available; paid plans for more credits.

what_is: Apollo.io is a sales intelligence platform built around a database of business contacts and companies. You search by role, industry, company size, or location, then pull verified emails and phone numbers for the people you find. It also includes tools for email sequencing, call tracking, and enriching existing CRM records with current contact details.

Most operators use it simply for the contact database: build a list, then export or sync it into whatever outreach tool runs the actual emails. A free tier gives limited monthly credits, and paid plans raise that cap.
tools.who_for: Apollo.io is one of the more approachable tools in this directory. There is no code and no setup beyond creating an account: if you can use filters and export a spreadsheet, you can build a working prospect list. The one thing to manage is credits: free plans cap how many contacts you can reveal each month, so a tightly targeted list goes further than a broad one.

tools.name: Apify
tools.tagline: Web scraping and automation platform, including a Google Maps scraper.
ai4ntp_take: Alec Saluga used Apify's free-tier Google Maps scraper in Episode 002 to pull a prospect list by keyword and geo while testing how fast an AI-built company could source real leads. He came back to Apify in Episode 004, one of seven under-the-radar AI tools featured that episode, this time using its off-the-shelf scrapers for social platforms to pull data for prospecting instead of coding a custom crawler.

We keep Apify in the stack because the Google Maps Actor does, for free, what tools like Apollo.io charge for: a targeted list of businesses by category and location, ready to plug into outreach. The practical takeaway is that you don't need a developer or a paid data provider to build a first prospect list. You need a keyword, a place, and a few minutes on Apify's free tier before you decide whether a paid tool is worth it.

episodes.used_for: Scraping prospect lists from Google Maps by keyword and geo
Pulling social platform data with off-the-shelf scrapers
Scraping an attorney lead list to feed the cold-email agent

pricing.summary: Free tier available; usage-based paid plans.

what_is: Apify is a cloud platform for web scraping, browser automation, and pulling data for AI projects. Instead of writing your own scraping code, you can run any of more than 35,000 ready-made tools, called Actors, that pull data from sites like Google Maps and other platforms.

You pick an Actor, enter what you're looking for, such as a keyword, a location, or a profile URL, and it returns structured data you can export as a spreadsheet or feed into another tool. Apify also lets developers build custom scrapers and automation workflows when the pre-made ones don't fit.
tools.who_for: Apify itself is a developer platform underneath. It's built around code templates, APIs, and configurable scraping jobs, and the full toolset assumes some technical comfort. But the specific use we recommend, running the pre-built Google Maps Actor, is simple: you enter a search term and a location and download a list, no code required. Treat Apify as a tool where you use one packaged feature rather than the platform at large, and leave the rest of it to the developers.

tools.name: Fireshot
tools.tagline: Chrome extension that captures full-page screenshots in one click.
ai4ntp_take: On Episode 002, Alec Saluga used a trick he picked up from Ian: screenshot an entire site with Fireshot and feed it into an AI prompt as context, while building GotoBuild, an AI workout planner, in under an hour. He reached for the same move on Episode 005, capturing a full Crumbl reference page and dropping it straight into the prompt while building Milk and Cookies, a bakery brand, as one of three real companies built live in that episode.

The takeaway: when you want an AI tool to match a website's look, layout, or brand feel, screenshotting the whole page and pasting it into the prompt gives the model far more to work with than a written description ever could. Fireshot stays in the stack because it turns "make it look like this" into something you can actually show the AI, not just tell it. For an operator, that's the appeal: one click, no design software, no code, and a reference image ready to paste straight into your next prompt.

episodes.used_for: Capturing whole sites as AI context
Capturing a full Crumbl reference page as prompt context

pricing.summary: Free; paid Pro for advanced exports.

what_is: Fireshot is a Chrome extension that captures full-page screenshots of any website in a single click, including everything below the fold that would normally require scrolling. It saves the capture as an image or PDF and lets you annotate, crop, or mark it up before exporting.

It's built for anyone who needs a complete visual record of a webpage rather than just what's visible on screen. The free version covers standard capture and export; a paid Pro tier adds more advanced editing and export options.
tools.who_for: Fireshot is about as approachable as browser tools get. You click the extension icon, it captures the full page automatically, and you export or copy the image right away; there's no setup and nothing to configure. For an operator building with AI, that makes it genuinely useful: capture a competitor's site or a brand reference page and hand it to your AI tool as visual context, no design or coding skill required.

tools.name: PLAUD Note
tools.tagline: Pocket AI voice recorder that captures meetings and calls.
ai4ntp_take: Alec Saluga brought PLAUD Note on screen as the hardware recorder in his stack. It snaps onto the back of his phone, so it is always on hand for the calls and in-person meetings that never get scheduled through a laptop. Once a conversation is recorded, PLAUD routes the transcript out through Zapier. In one setup, the recordings land in Google Drive, and from there Claude reads the transcript and turns it into notes, summaries, or next steps, no manual copying or typing required.

We keep PLAUD Note in the stack because it solves the part of AI workflows most people skip: getting the conversation into text in the first place. A phone can record audio, but a dedicated device that hands off cleanly to Zapier removes a step. The practical takeaway for you is that the value is not in the recorder itself, it is in what happens after: a plumbing setup that turns a recorded meeting into something your AI tools can actually read and act on.

episodes.used_for: Capturing meetings and calls into an AI workflow
Routing recordings into Google Drive via Zapier for Claude to read

pricing.summary: Hardware purchase plus an app subscription; see site.

what_is: PLAUD Note is a pocket-sized AI voice recorder that clips onto the back of your phone or slips into a pocket. It records in-person conversations, phone calls, and online meetings, then transcribes and summarizes them with AI so you get a searchable record of what was said.

PLAUD says it bridges these three capture modes (in person, phone, online) into a single workflow, and reports more than 2 million users worldwide. PLAUD Note pairs with a companion app and integrates with tools like Zapier, so recordings can flow automatically into other software for further processing.
tools.who_for: PLAUD Note itself is built for anyone: charge it, clip it on, hit record, and the app hands you a transcript. No coding, no dev setup. Where it gets more advanced is the automation layer AI4NTP built around it, routing recordings through Zapier into Google Drive and Claude. You do not need to build that yourself to get value from the recorder, but if you want the full hands-off pipeline, plan on setting up Zapier once or asking someone who has.

tools.name: Loom
tools.tagline: Async video messaging for handoffs, sales, and coaching.
ai4ntp_take: In Episode 001, on how marketers are actually using AI to grow, the operators called out Loom as the one tool in the stack that isn't AI at all. They use it for the parts of the job that video still handles better than a doc or a Slack thread: handing off a project so the next person can see exactly what you mean instead of reading a paragraph of instructions, walking a prospect through a proposal without booking a call, and coaching a teammate by recording over their shoulder and talking through the fix.

We keep it in the stack because AI speeds up what you produce, but it doesn't replace showing your work. A two-minute recording of a dashboard, a workflow, or a piece of feedback lands faster than a written explanation and doesn't require the other person to be free at the same moment you are. The practical takeaway: when an explanation would take three paragraphs to write, hit record instead and send the link.

episodes.used_for: Async handoffs, sales, and coaching

pricing.summary: Free tier available; paid plans for teams.

what_is: Loom is a screen and camera recording tool for sending video messages in place of a meeting or a long email. You hit record, capture your screen, your face, or both, and Loom turns it into a shareable link you can send instead of scheduling a call.

It's built around async communication: instead of a live call, you send a video someone can watch, pause, and comment on whenever it fits their schedule. Loom offers a free tier for individuals and paid plans for teams that add more storage, editing tools, and viewer analytics. It's widely used for handoffs, walkthroughs, sales follow-ups, and coaching.
tools.who_for: Loom is one of the easier tools in this directory. There's nothing to configure and nothing AI-specific to learn: install the extension or app, click record, and you get a link to send. It's a good fit for anyone who explains things over Slack or email today and wants a faster, clearer way to do it, whether that's handing off work, walking a client through something, or giving feedback without scheduling a call.

tools.name: Namecheap
tools.tagline: Domain registrar and hosting.
ai4ntp_take: Ian Kilpatrick used it in Episode 002, "We built a company with AI in under an hour," to buy the domain for the episode's voted brief, GotoBuild (an AI workout planner), live on camera, for one cent, on one of Namecheap's promo deals. He prefers Namecheap over GoDaddy, saying it runs at roughly 60% of GoDaddy's price for a comparable domain.

Why we keep it in the stack: buying a domain is often the first real step that turns a brief into a live product, and Namecheap makes that step fast and cheap enough to do on camera in under an hour. The practical takeaway: you don't need a developer to register a domain. Search, claim it, and hand it off to whoever builds your site next.

episodes.used_for: Buying the GotoBuild domain live for one cent

pricing.summary: Paid; domains from ~$1 on promo (stated on the live).

what_is: Namecheap is a domain name registrar and web hosting company. It lets you search for, buy, and manage domain names, the web address people type to find your site, along with services like web hosting, SSL certificates, and email hosting. It's one of the larger domain registrars, known for competitive pricing and frequent promotions on new domain purchases.

Once you own a domain through Namecheap, you point it at wherever your site actually lives, a hosting provider, a page builder, or a platform like Vercel, and manage renewals, DNS settings, and privacy protection from one dashboard.
tools.who_for: Namecheap is one of the easier tools in this stack to use on your own. Searching for and buying a domain takes a few clicks and no code, which is why Ian bought GotoBuild's domain live on camera during a build session. It gets more technical right after purchase, when you point the domain at your hosting or app through DNS settings, a step that's easy to get wrong. If that part worries you, buy the domain yourself and hand the DNS setup to whoever builds your site.

tools.name: Instant Domain Search
tools.tagline: Instant domain-availability search.
ai4ntp_take: In Episode 002, where the team built a company with AI in under an hour, Ian Kilpatrick used Instant Domain Search to check whether gotobuild.pro was available for their AI workout planner idea. Rather than guessing and finding out only after committing to the name, he typed it in, saw it was open, and moved straight to buying it, live during the episode.

We keep it in the stack because naming a company or an app moves fast when the domain check doesn't slow you down. The instant results let you test several name ideas back to back without losing momentum, and the extension list surfaces options beyond .com that you might not have considered. The practical takeaway for any operator: check availability before you fall in love with a name, not after.

episodes.used_for: Checking domain availability live before buying gotobuild.pro

pricing.summary: Free.

what_is: Instant Domain Search is a domain name search tool that checks availability across hundreds of domain extensions in real time. Type a name and it shows, almost instantly, which versions are free to register (.com, .io, .ai, and hundreds more) alongside pricing, so you can compare options without checking each registrar one by one.
It's built for speed: results appear as you type, rather than after a page reload. Once you find a name you like, you can move straight to buying it through a registrar.
tools.who_for: Instant Domain Search is about as easy as tools get: you type a business or project name into a search box and see, instantly, which domain extensions are free and how much they cost. There's no signup needed to search, and no technical setup. If you can type a name and read a price, you can use it. It's a good fit the moment you're naming anything, a company, a product, a side project, and want to know before you commit.

tools.name: DigitalOcean
tools.tagline: Cloud hosting with simple virtual servers (droplets) and an in-browser console.
what_is: DigitalOcean is a cloud infrastructure provider. It rents out virtual private servers, called droplets, along with managed databases and storage, so anyone can run software on a machine that lives in a data center instead of on their own computer.

It's known for simple, transparent pricing, with plans starting at a few dollars a month, and for tooling built to be approachable, including an in-browser console that lets you manage a server without installing anything locally. That makes it a common starting point for hosting a website, an app, or an AI agent that needs to run continuously.
ai4ntp_take: Ian used DigitalOcean live in Episode 003 to give an OpenClaw AI agent a real home instead of a laptop that has to stay open. He spun up a droplet (DigitalOcean's term for a virtual private server), a roughly 18-dollar-a-month box with 2GB of memory, and ran the whole setup, install, configuration, and hardening, straight from DigitalOcean's in-browser console. No local terminal or SSH client required to follow along.

Before the agent went live, Ian locked the droplet down with fail2ban and a firewall so it wasn't sitting open on the internet. That's the real lesson: a cheap server is easy to get, but a few minutes of hardening is what makes it safe to leave running. We keep DigitalOcean in the stack because it's the fastest way to move an agent off a laptop and onto a real server, at a price that's easy to predict.

episodes.used_for: Hosting the live OpenClaw agent on a 2GB VPS droplet

pricing.summary: Droplets from a few dollars a month; about 18 dollars a month for the 2GB server used on the live.

tools.who_for: DigitalOcean is server infrastructure, and setting one up (choosing a droplet, configuring a firewall, hardening it against attacks) is a technical job. If you're not comfortable at a command line, this isn't something you sit down and click through on your own. Where it fits for you is one step removed: it's what a developer, or an AI agent working under your direction, uses to give your project a real, always-on home. Worth understanding, even if you never touch it yourself.

tools.name: Telegram
tools.tagline: A free messaging app with a bot platform (BotFather) for connecting automated agents.
what_is: Telegram is a free messaging app for phone, desktop, and browser, similar to WhatsApp or iMessage but built around large groups and channels.

Its bigger draw for builders is BotFather, a chat-based tool built into Telegram for creating bots through its Bot API. Anyone can register a bot in a few messages and get a token that lets a program send and receive messages through it, which is why Telegram has become a common front end for chatting with AI agents.
ai4ntp_take: In Episode 003, Ian Kilpatrick used Telegram as the control channel for a fresh OpenClaw agent. He walked through BotFather to register a new bot in a few messages, wired it to the agent, and from there could message the agent directly from his phone to assign it tasks. No dashboard, no separate app to check, just a chat thread.

We keep Telegram in the stack because it turns an agent into something you actually check. Everyone already has a messaging app open all day, so putting your agent there means you catch what it's doing and can redirect it in real time. Ian calls it his favorite channel because BotFather is the easiest setup we've used: no approval wait, no developer account, just a conversation with a bot that hands you a token. If you want to control an agent from your phone without opening a browser, this is a quick way to do it.

episodes.used_for: Connected the OpenClaw agent so you control it by chat
Provisioning a personal agent for each employee via the BotFather (SaucyBot)

pricing.summary: Free.

tools.who_for: Telegram itself is as easy as any messaging app: download it, make an account, start chatting. Where it gets more technical is BotFather, the tool that creates the bot and hands you an API token to wire into your agent. That step is a one-time setup a developer or your AI builder handles for you. Once it's connected, using it is just texting, which is why it's a good fit even if you never touch a line of code.

tools.name: Tailscale
tools.tagline: A mesh VPN built on WireGuard that privately connects your devices and servers.
what_is: Tailscale is a mesh VPN built on the WireGuard protocol. It connects your devices and servers into a private network so they can talk to each other directly, without opening ports on the public internet or routing traffic through a third party. Install it on a laptop, a home server, and a cloud instance, and Tailscale handles the encryption and routing so the machines behave as if they're on the same local network, no matter where they physically sit.
Setup is largely automatic: sign in, install the app, and devices join the network on their own. Tailscale offers a free tier for personal use and paid plans for teams.
ai4ntp_take: Ian Kilpatrick uses Tailscale to keep server-side agent work private. In Episode 003, building an AI clone agent with OpenClaw, he ran a Tailscale funnel between his own machine and the servers doing the heavy lifting, so the connection stayed off the public internet. That mattered because the work involved competitive or financial data he did not want exposed, even briefly, to anything outside his own network.

We keep Tailscale in the stack because agent work increasingly runs on remote servers, not just local scripts, raising the stakes on what stays public versus private. A mesh VPN closes that gap without the operator managing firewall rules or open ports. The practical takeaway: if an agent setup touches sensitive data and needs a server in the loop, that connection should not sit on the open internet. You do not need to configure Tailscale yourself to benefit from this lesson, but you should ask whether your agent's traffic is private before it handles anything sensitive.

episodes.used_for: Running a private VPN funnel to keep sensitive agent work off the open internet

pricing.summary: Free tier for personal use; paid plans for teams.

tools.who_for: Tailscale is a technical tool. Setting it up involves installing software on servers, understanding network concepts, and making decisions about routing and access. If that is not something you do yourself, it is not a tool you need to run. But it is worth understanding what it does: if you or your team ever run agents on a remote server with sensitive data involved, ask whoever built it whether that connection is private.

tools.name: Firecrawl
tools.tagline: An API that crawls websites and returns clean, LLM-ready data.
what_is: A web scraping and crawling API that turns websites into clean markdown or structured data for AI applications. It handles JavaScript rendering and anti-bot measures, so developers or the AI agents they build don't have to fight broken HTML or blocked requests.

Point it at a URL or a whole site, and it returns content formatted for language models to read. That makes it useful for feeding research, SEO audits, or any workflow where an AI needs to ingest web pages at scale.
ai4ntp_take: In Episode 003, Ian Kilpatrick used Firecrawl to build an AI Clone Agent with OpenClaw. The agent needed to read a lot of pages to do its job, so Firecrawl handled the scraping: pulling pages at scale and handing back clean data the agent could actually use for research and SEO audits, instead of raw HTML full of ads, nav bars, and scripts.

We keep it in the stack because it is the piece that solves a problem every agent eventually hits: the open web is messy, and most sites actively resist being scraped. Firecrawl handles that mess once so you don't have to solve it inside every agent you build. The practical takeaway: you don't need to know how it works under the hood. When someone builds you an agent that needs to "read the internet," Firecrawl is likely the tool doing that reading behind the scenes.

episodes.used_for: Scraping pages at scale to feed the agent

pricing.summary: Free tier with credits; usage-based paid plans.

tools.who_for: Firecrawl is a developer tool. It's an API, which means you use it by writing code or plugging it into an agent framework, not by clicking around a dashboard. If you're not building agents yourself, you likely won't touch Firecrawl directly, but you don't need to: whoever builds your AI agent (or the AI you're working with) can wire it in to handle the web-reading piece. Worth knowing it exists, and worth asking for by name when you want an agent that can research or audit pages at scale.

tools.name: Kimi (Moonshot AI)
tools.tagline: A large language model from Moonshot AI, positioned as a low-cost option.
what_is: Kimi is a family of large language models built by Moonshot AI. It's available through a chat interface and an API, and it's best known for two things: long context windows that let it handle large amounts of text in a single prompt, and pricing that runs well below many of the bigger-name models.

For anyone comparing model options, Kimi sits in the low-cost tier alongside models like ChatGPT or Claude. It's useful when you need to process a lot of text or run a model at volume without a lot of budget.
ai4ntp_take: Ian ran Kimi as one of the model brains behind OpenClaw, the AI clone agent he built in Episode 003. The appeal was straightforward: it's cheap to run, and cheap matters when an agent is making a lot of calls. Over time, Ian found the outputs got inconsistent, so he moved on to a different model. Kimi came back in Episode 004, where it became the cheap model option Justin switched to inside Hermes while testing lesser-known AI tools.

We keep Kimi in the stack because cost is a real variable when you're running agents at volume, and Kimi is one of the clearest low-cost options to test against. The practical takeaway for an operator: don't assume the cheapest model is the wrong call, test it, but watch for drift. If a model's answers start getting less reliable the longer you run it, that's your cue to switch, which is what happened when Ian used it for OpenClaw.

episodes.used_for: Tried as a low-cost model brain for OpenClaw
Serving as the cheap model swapped into Hermes

pricing.summary: Usage-based API pricing; described as a cheap option on the live.

tools.who_for: Kimi is approachable if you just want to open its chat interface and ask questions; it works like any other AI chatbot. Where it gets technical is the API, which is how AI4NTP actually uses it: as a model brain plugged into agents like OpenClaw or Hermes. Most operators won't touch that layer directly. You don't need to know how to wire up Kimi's API to benefit from it. You just need to know it exists as a cheap option when you or your builder are choosing which model powers an agent.

tools.name: Claude (Anthropic)
tools.tagline: Anthropic’s family of AI models, used as an assistant and as an agent’s model brain.
what_is: Claude is Anthropic's family of AI models. You can use it directly through the Claude app at claude.ai, or developers can access it through Anthropic's API to build it into other software. People use it for writing, research, analysis, and coding, and it also serves as the reasoning engine behind AI agents, tools that plan and carry out multi-step tasks on their own.
Anthropic offers Claude in several model sizes, from fast and lightweight to more capable, so the same assistant can handle quick everyday questions or longer, more complex work.
ai4ntp_take: Ian ran Claude (Opus 4.6) as the model brain behind his original OpenClaw agent for his first ten weeks building with AI, and he loved it. In Episode 003 he explained switching away after Anthropic blocked OpenClaw from using subscription tokens. Claude also does real production work on the show: Claude Design built the Slow Hand Remodeling and AI4NTP sites from mockups, and in Episode 005 the team used it to ideate brands, rebuild Milk and Cookies, write copy, and critique Sunday Crumb design passes.

We keep Claude in the stack because it holds up across different jobs, from powering an agent's reasoning to production writing and design work you can ship to clients. The practical takeaway: you do not need to pick just one AI to learn. Claude is worth having open alongside whatever agent or tool you are building with, since it is often the model doing the real thinking behind the scenes.

episodes.used_for: Powering his original OpenClaw agent's model brain (Opus 4.6)
Building the Slow Hand Remodeling and AI4NTP sites from mockups

episodes.operator: Alec Saluga, Justin Novak, and Ian Kilpatrick
episodes.used_for: Ideating brands, rebuilding Milk and Cookies, writing copy, and critiquing Sunday Crumb design passes
Brainstorming and building the first version of the Saints Classical LMS

pricing.summary: Free tier; Pro around 20 dollars a month; API is usage-based.

tools.who_for: Claude is easy to pick up on the assistant side: chatting with it, or asking it to write, research, or edit, works like ChatGPT through the app at claude.ai, and it is free to start. It gets more technical when Claude is the model running inside an agent or another piece of software, since that goes through Anthropic's API. In that case, a developer or your AI agent is the one calling it, not you typing into a box. You do not need to touch the API yourself to benefit from it.

tools.name: GitHub
tools.tagline: The world’s largest platform for hosting and collaborating on code with Git.
what_is: GitHub is the world's largest platform for hosting and collaborating on code using Git, the version control system developers rely on to track every change to a project. It stores your code in repositories, where you can see the full history of edits, revert mistakes, and work alongside others without overwriting each other's work.

Beyond storage, GitHub adds tools for collaboration: pull requests for reviewing changes before they merge, issues for tracking bugs and tasks, and GitHub Actions for automating steps like testing or deployment. It's free for public and private repositories, with paid Team and Enterprise plans for larger organizations.
ai4ntp_take: GitHub is where the code our agents write actually lands. In Episode 003, Ian Kilpatrick showed how an agent can push what it builds straight to a repo, so you get a real, versioned copy of the code instead of a black box you have to trust. That episode also used GitHub to host OpenClaw's open-source versions, so anyone building their own AI clone agent has a working codebase to start from. In Episode 005, Alec Saluga pointed to GitHub from the other direction: it's the normal code handoff layer that a tool like Lovable can skip entirely, publishing a working app without ever making you touch a repository.

We keep GitHub in the stack because it's the ground truth for anything an agent builds. Even if you never open a terminal, knowing your code lives in a real, versioned repository (not trapped inside one tool) means you can always move it, hand it to a developer, or roll back a bad change.

episodes.used_for: Hosting the code agents write and OpenClaw's open source versions
Explaining the code handoff layer Lovable skips for first publish

pricing.summary: Free for public and private repos; paid Team and Enterprise plans.

tools.who_for: GitHub itself is a developer tool, built around Git commands, branches, and pull requests that assume some coding background. You don't need to learn any of that to benefit from it here: the agents you build with push their own code to GitHub, and Ian and Alec both treat it as the place that code lives once it's written. It's worth knowing it exists and what a repository is, even if you never open one yourself.

tools.name: SendGrid
tools.tagline: Email delivery service for sending transactional and marketing email at scale.
what_is: SendGrid is a cloud-based email delivery service, owned by Twilio, built for sending transactional and marketing email at scale. Developers connect to it through an API or SMTP to send things like password resets, receipts, order confirmations, and bulk newsletters without running their own mail servers.

It handles the parts that make email actually land in an inbox: deliverability tuning, sender reputation management, and analytics on opens, clicks, and bounces. Most people never see SendGrid directly. It works in the background every time an app, website, or automation sends them an email.
ai4ntp_take: In Episode 003, Ian Kilpatrick used SendGrid to send bulk email at scale while building an AI clone agent with OpenClaw. That episode became AI4NTP's most-repeated security lesson: a single exposed SendGrid API key sent 127,000 emails from Ian's own address.

We still use SendGrid for scale sending because it does the job well, but every key gets guarded and rotated, never pasted into a script and forgotten. If you or an AI agent connects to SendGrid on your behalf, know where that key lives and who can see it. A leaked key does not just risk spam, it can send six figures of email under your name before anyone notices.

episodes.used_for: Sent bulk email at scale; the source of Ian's API-key security lesson

pricing.summary: Free tier with limited daily sends; usage-based paid plans.

tools.who_for: SendGrid is a developer tool. Sending mail through it means calling an API or configuring SMTP settings, not clicking a button in a dashboard, so it isn't something you would set up solo without engineering help. In practice, a developer or an AI agent wires it up on your behalf. It's still worth understanding the API-key risk even if you never touch the integration, because a leaked key can send bulk email as you before anyone notices.

tools.name: GoHighLevel
tools.tagline: All-in-one CRM and marketing platform for agencies and growing businesses.
what_is: GoHighLevel is an all-in-one sales and marketing CRM built for agencies and growing service businesses. It combines sales pipelines, funnel and landing page builders, email and SMS automation, calendars, and reputation management into one platform, in place of stitching together a handful of separate tools.

The idea is to run an entire revenue operation from one system: track leads as they move through a pipeline, automate follow-up messages, book appointments, and manage customer reviews, all without switching apps.
ai4ntp_take: Justin has run his businesses on GoHighLevel for about 10 years, using it as the backbone of revenue operations: sales pipelines, follow-up automation, and AI-assisted workflows. In Episode 004, "7 AI tools you have never heard of," he demoed that live, querying James's pipeline by voice and getting the answer straight from the CRM.

That is the real test for a CRM this sprawling: can an AI agent actually read your pipeline data and answer questions about it. GoHighLevel passes, which is why it stays in the stack even though it is overkill if all you need is a newsletter tool. For a real sales motion, meaning leads, follow-up, and appointments that need tracking, it is the tool we reach for. The visit link on this page is an AI4NTP affiliate link, and we would point you to GoHighLevel either way.

episodes.used_for: Querying James's pipeline by voice in a live agentic CRM demo

pricing.summary: 14-day free trial (start it through AI4NTP). Plans from about $97/mo (Starter) to $297/mo (Unlimited) to $497/mo (Pro).

tools.who_for: GoHighLevel is not a technical tool in the coding sense, you do not need to write code to use it, but it is not a lightweight one either. It is a full platform with a real learning curve: pipelines, automations, funnels, and calendars all need setting up before it pays off. If you are running an actual sales process, with leads to track and follow-up to automate, it is worth the setup time. If you just need to send a newsletter, it will feel like overkill.

tools.name: HyperFrames
tools.tagline: Open-source framework that turns HTML, CSS, and GSAP compositions into deterministic MP4 video, built for AI agents.
what_is: HyperFrames is an open-source framework for building video with code instead of a timeline editor. You write compositions in HTML, CSS, and GSAP (a JavaScript animation library), and HyperFrames renders them into a real MP4 file. Because the process is deterministic, the same code always produces the exact same video, frame for frame.

It's built specifically for AI agents. Instead of a person clicking through a manual video editor, an agent can write and run the code that generates the video, making it possible to produce and reproduce videos programmatically at scale.
ai4ntp_take: AI4NTP hasn't run HyperFrames through a live build yet. We're flagging it as an editor's pick because it fits squarely into our core thesis: let agents do the work instead of a person operating software by hand. HyperFrames proves that HTML can double as a video format, which means an agent that already knows how to write HTML, CSS, and a bit of GSAP can generate a finished, repeatable video straight from code, without touching a timeline or exporting by hand.

We keep it in the stack because it's open source, so there's no cost or lock-in risk in testing it against a real use case. The practical takeaway for an operator: you don't need to touch the code yourself. If your team or an AI agent can output HTML, you now have a path to generating video programmatically instead of building it by hand in a template tool.

pricing.summary: Free and open source.

tools.who_for: HyperFrames is a developer tool at its core: using it directly means writing HTML, CSS, and GSAP animation code, not clicking through a template. If you don't write code, this isn't something you'll operate yourself. Where it matters to you is as infrastructure an AI agent or a technical teammate can run on your behalf to generate video automatically. Worth knowing about even if you never open the code, since it's the kind of tool that lets an agent replace a manual video-editing workflow.

tools.name: n8n
tools.tagline: Open-source, visual workflow automation you can self-host.
what_is: n8n is a source-available workflow automation tool built around a visual, node-based editor. Instead of writing code, you drag and connect nodes representing apps, APIs, and AI models onto a canvas to build automated pipelines, things like moving data between tools, scraping information on a schedule, or triggering an action whenever something changes.

You can run n8n on its own cloud service or self-host it on your own server. Self-hosting is what sets it apart from tools like Zapier: instead of paying per task the automation runs, you pay a flat server cost and run it as often as you like.
ai4ntp_take: Alec Saluga picked n8n on Episode 004. He builds client automations in it because every step of a workflow is visual and debuggable, you can see exactly where data is flowing and catch the point where something breaks. He demoed a live example: a workflow that scrapes a client's YouTube, Instagram, and TikTok follower counts every Sunday and pushes the updated numbers straight to their website, no manual reporting involved.

We keep n8n in the stack because it is open source, so you self-host it for a few dollars a month in server costs and run it as many times as you want, instead of paying per execution the way tools like Zapier charge. That makes it cheap to lean on heavily once a client relationship grows. Alec calls it the best place to start before graduating to something like Claude Code: you learn how automations are actually structured on a visual canvas, then move to writing your own once you outgrow it.

episodes.used_for: Scraping live creator stats and pushing them to a client's website

pricing.summary: Open source and self-hostable for free; paid Cloud plans available.

tools.who_for: n8n is one of the more approachable tools here. You are not writing code, you are dragging nodes onto a canvas and connecting them, so if you can follow a flowchart you can build a real automation. The one technical piece is self-hosting: running your own instance takes some basic server setup, though n8n's cloud plan skips that step entirely. Chaining more than a couple of steps together still takes some patience to learn.

tools.name: Higgsfield
tools.tagline: Run one prompt through many image and video models in one place.
